sentences.text: No.
It was-- there was no wall.
It was just ordinarily as it was before.
We were just had to muck in with other family.
Maybe some people had to pay a little bit of rent to strangers.
Not everybody had family in this-- in this village where the ghetto was.
It was only a large-- larger village than the others.
And I believe that they were-- there were not touched.
Like the Romanian people were not checked out of their houses to make room for us, like they did in other ghettos.
There was no fences, no nothing.
You could walk out.
But nobody did, because we were just content to sit-- wait to sit, and what happened-- see what happened.

And there-- there was-- I mean, I remember my father was allowed to go home to the-- because when they had taken us away, they boarded-- they boarded up the front doors and all that with board-- boarding, fencing.
And as far as I know, they said, we don't see why not.
But actually he was not allowed to walk out just like that.
He had permission to go.
But we just carried on more or less as we were in our own little village, playing about-- playing about, kicking about, whatever kids played with.
Yes, we did.
We had family because-- we had family in two or three villages, because they were in-- my father's family consisted of about six or eight brothers and sisters.
And if they didn't live in our village they lived in the next village, so on.
So there was always room to muck in.
And-- and they had great big rooms.
And there was-- all-- all the houses were on one floor.
It was sort of-- everybody just managed to do their-- whatever they need-- needed it.
Needed doing.
But then, as I said, they came along and-- and sort of more or less-- I don't know what you call it.
Round up.
They just said all men to report.
And by the morning, we were allowed to go back for half hour and join our-- the females, Mom and Dad.
Moms and all that.
And we were sort of joined up again with families, and everybody carried whatever they could on their shoulders, rucksacks and suitcases.
And we were taken off to the train, which was in Felsoviso, I might add.
There was a Viseu, and there was a Felsoviso.
I don't know why they called it that.
And that's where there-- it was the nearest train to us, which was about three hours, maybe more, walking distance.
At least three hours, year.
We was passing two or three villages to get to this larger-- where the trains were running.
We had these guards.
As I say, there was very few in between.
I mean, on hindsight when you think about it, you could have just dropped back.
You could have just run away and hid.
I mean, we didn't know.
We heard the guns-- the Russian guns was already firing at night.
We heard them.
I mean, nobody was even thinking about that because we were supposed to go on and be better off, because we going to work, and we going to look-- be looked after, and all the rest of it.
If you knew then what you knew-- what we know now, at least 15%, 20% could have run away, hidden.
They had no way to find us, to catch us, unless they called help from other towns, and all the rest of it.
And we was just going like lambs to a slaughterhouse, as far as I'm concerned today.
No, it was three hours, or three to four hours.
Yeah.
Because I remember we caught-- we got the train late afternoon.
It was a cattle train.
There was sort of no seats.
There was-- I think there was straw on the floor.
There was no seating.
There was just-- everybody sort of found themselves-- their selves a little corner.
And that's it.
I remember when people used to have to go to-- to do their nature, it was in a corner behind a curtain they strung up.
And I think they was too stupid to realize what was happening.
They didn't want to know, if they did.
I should imagine about 150.
The first time I really-- we realized what was happening, is I had a-- a very frum uncle with a white beard.
He-- just like Father Christmas.
And he started praying half a day or so before we actually arrived in Auschwitz.
We arrived at night, by the way.
And they just opened the doors and they said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
You didn't know where you was.
There was bright lights shining on-- on you, and dogs running around with their handlers, barking.
And still didn't know what was happening.
I mean, you could smell burning flesh.
You could smell the gas chambers.
Who would have even dream of-- about these things?
I mean, there was cleverer people than sort of like, say, me.
And I didn't even know nothing.
Why I survived is because I was just too young to realize what was happening.
I didn't know what day it was.
I didn't know-- we were all numbers.
No names.
My number was 54990.
I had it on my jacket.
On my jacket, on the striped jacket.
And that's how it was.
By the way, when you arrive in Auschwitz, if you stayed in Auschwitz you got a number on your-- a tattoo on your arm.
But if you was transported, within a week, you just got a number on your clothes.
And I'm the one who got a number on my clothes because I was transferred to Buchenwald.
This train journey-- to think I remember exactly.
Probably not short of-- not far short of three days.
Probably three nights and two days, I would say.
Yes, all--
--all together.
We were all together.
All together.
All the families stuck-- they-- they stuck together.
Because they let you believe that you are what you are-- what they said.
You are going to work, and you live in a-- houses, and you live in families.
And you-- they going to work in the fields, on the farms, because their menfolk are fighting in the front and they can't--and that's why they need labor.
No.

They were just-- just obviously-- I mean, it was crowded and people were, you know-- we were-- also we were broad minded, and it didn't mean nothing about people going to the loo and all that and hiding.
But in cases like that, you were so crowded in because there was just impossible for people to act normal.
So gradually, as the hours were going on, some people start panicking.
There was faintings.
And I think-- I'm not sure-- I think there was even one or two deaths as well before we arrived on it-- onto Auschwitz.
Of course, which we didn't know where we were going to and all the rest of it.
Did you get any food or water?
We didn't get any food or water, but we took with us.
We took-- we had our own.
And as I was saying before, it was more or less rationed by our parents because they didn't know when they would be able to--
--have another lot.
And at the end of the day when we arrived there, everything was left on the train.
And I remember I was really being angry because all that food which we should have ate was saved for a rainy day.
And unfortunately, the rainy day came and it was left there on the train.
I didn't really have any impression.
I-- I was very frightened because people were-- they opened these big sort of sliding doors.
And there was dogs barking and people shouting, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
There were some people dressed in-- in striped uniforms.
And it was about midnight, 1 o'clock in the morning.
And you was just like sheep.
Go there, go here.
And you did what you was told.
Then we finished up going in front of a sort of a long, long line of people.
They saw-- shouted men separate, women separate.
As far as we were concerned, even a two-year-old is a woman.
So we divided.
And we went through selection.
My brother was tall-- a tall man.
He-- they came along with a walking stick and pulled him out so he didn't even go through selection.
When me and my father and my younger brother came along, I believe my younger brother saved me because my father was a tall person.
And I was a twin.
And I was really small.
But against my younger brother, I was a big boy.
So they told him, you'll stay-- you're going back to your mother.
And when they asked me what I was doing, I said I was working with pelts, which was fur and all the rest of it.
And I was selected to go-- right or left, I don't know which.
And my father was-- mark me.
I was together with my father.
And then we were taken sort of to blocks.
And then we met up again with my brother.
There were three of us together.
There were-- no.
That was the brother who was picked out with a walking stick.
Round his neck.
Younger brother-- my younger brother I have never seen after that, once they took him away and said-- there was another 12 or 15 kids there.
And said he's going back to his mother.
And as far as-- I've never seen him since.
We met up with my older brother, with Chaim.
And we were-- the two of us and my father were together for less than a week.
Because every day, they called out different people and took them off to different parts of Germany, different camps.
I-- I read since books-- I couldn't help it.
Every time I used to go to library-- I still do-- I pick up books about the war.

I read that just that particular time when our transports arrived, about 60% of the people were chosen for work because they were-- they suddenly realized they killed too many before, and they wanted labor.
I don't think I would have been picked for work because I was only a nothing.
I was a little boy or 14, not even.
And they-- they chose me to work.
And I've read since that 55% to 60% were chosen for work.
Previously there was less than 35%.
So that is-- what can I say.
A bit of luck in our respect for our villages from where we came from, our part of Romania.
As far-- as far as I know, that's what I read.
Whether it's true-- or must be true if it was in the library, in the books and all that.
So-- So the first night you were there, and you were taken to--
I was together-- it was two or three nights.
We were together in the Block 55, which was right down at the bottom of Buchenwald-- of Auschwitz.
We were taken-- my brother was called out first, about three days later.
I've not-- I have not seen him until first time we met in '55.
That what I'm talking about, '44.
Or 11 years I've never-- I haven't seen him.
My father was called out about three-- two or three days later, and I was left on my own.
I remember I was a very fussy eater, and my father was begging me to eat the soup.
And it was ghastly.
It was horrible.
And he said, you won't get another one here.
You got to eat it.
It's not-- you're not at home.
And I was nearly sick, just drank that soup down like I gulped it down.
And then as I said, two or three days later my father was called out and taken away.
And I was left on my own.
And then a week, 10 days later, I myself was taken all the way to Buchenwald.
And I finished up Block 8 in Buchenwald, which was a children's block.
It was run-- run by a Russian kapo.
And it was-- from what I've heard from my friends talking about what they went through, it looked like it was heaven, where I was in Buchenwald.
Because I was appointed to carry the food for the-- the youngsters who went to work.
And I was sort of small, but I was strong and muscly.
And I-- and I didn't go-- I never worked in Buchenwald, but I was taken out-- I was picked out to take the soup twice a day, in the morning and in the evening before they came home from work.
And it was like dust pans.
So we was bringing this food for the workers when they came home, or in the morning before they were all woken up to get ready for work, which was probably 6 o'clock in the morning.
So I was lucky in that respect.
We-- we went by train.
It was-- just-- I don't exactly remember rightly how.
We was just taken by train to whole transport.
And they picked out a few of us children, and we were bunked into Block 8, which was-- a lot of Russian children was in that block-- blockade.
Yes, it was cattle trains.
As-- as-- as crowded as before.
And this time, we were-- we of course, obviously-- I forgot to mention that we were taken-- in Auschwitz we were taken to be deloused and shaved, and had to get in into a-- a vat.
And somehow or other, when I got in in this room, I didn't see anybody.
I saw people going in, and I didn't see anybody else.
I thought everybody jumps in there and they disappear.
So I got in this vat, and you had to sort of immerse in it.
And then you went in from this room into another room where you was dished out with these white stripes after being shaved and-- your head and your private parts, and all the rest of it.
And-- but what I did forget to say is our clothes.
They said, don't forget what your-- where-- where your clothes will be when you come back for them, and all the rest of it.
And I remember I had a treasured [? pen, ?] the first time I had a [? pen for ?] Pesach.
And it was given to me by my sister, who-- was strange, but she was giving it to me before Pesach.
And I-- so I-- I made sure that I knew exactly where my clothes are going to be.
But I never-- was never taken back to them clothes.
We can never see them again.
And from there, as I said, we met up-- my brother and my-- my father and myself.
And slowly, sort of daily they called out different transports.
And I lost both of them within about a week to 10 days, until I was called and I was taken on this transport to Buchenwald.
Yeah.
As-- as we arrived in Buchenwald, again, it was not like before.
Like [SPEAKING GERMAN], because they had you there.
And you was-- you had striped clothes, and you had shaved heads.
And you-- they were not frightened that you're going to run away or disappear or something.
We were just marched into five in a line, and we were marched off to the blocks.
And as I finished up with the rest of them in blocks, they was going right up to about 80.
I think I was this time in Block 67.
And they picked-- from there again they picked different people to-- in-- from Buchenwald itself, they were taking people to different parts of the country for work.
And I was picked to go to this [GERMAN], I call it today.
Block 8.
We were just a stone's throw from Appellplatz, where people used to have to stand for hours on end to be counted.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In row-- sometimes there was somebody missing, or maybe not and they miscounted.
And they used to have to stand out all night.
But we were again lucky because in this blockade, we were let in after the officer, the German officer, came to count us.
We were let in into the block because we learned in about two or three minutes-- we learned where our spot is.
And if they came to-- to count us again, we were virtually there as if we stayed there all the time.
So once again, it-- it's only because he was a very nice-- this guy was a Czech kapo.
I believe he was Czech.
And he was a very nice-- if there was somebody nice, he was nice there.
Because everybody was after everybody.
Nobody-- there was no friends, no nothing.
You just numbers.
And this particular person, he felt that-- that we could go indoors while they were-- somebody was missing or somebody got lost or some-- and we were lucky in that respect.
Food wasn't too bad.
It was short food, but it wasn't bad.
I remember we-- we-- they used to have a lot of German Jews in a couple of blocks in Buchenwald.

And they used to every now and then bring stale bread and stale cakes to us when-- to Block 8, and we used to have a good-- a good going over once a month or whatever it was, because we used to receive parcels from Switzerland and all these places, the German-- the German Jews.
I remember a funny thing.
One-- once before Christmas, we were taken, a few of us, to arrange chairs.
And the German Jewish prisoners were putting on a show for the officers.
And whilst we was there-- first time I've seen a show.
First time I've-- I never-- didn't know such a thing existed.
And obviously, it was like a farce like they have today.
And they put on a show.
And they were all clapping away and everything.
It was to me at that time-- you know, as I said, people were suffering very, very bad, very hard work, and all the rest.
And I was-- for some reason or other, I was chosen-- I was the lucky one.
I just carried the food twice a day from the kitchens, which was about three quarter-- half a mile, three quarter a mile from our blockade.
And in that respect, if you work with food, a little bit rubs on a-- on you.
And you're not as short as people who have been hard working for 8, 10, 12 hours a day.
Just loafing around.
We were-- we were playing [GERMAN].
We were playing-- I mean, I tried to learn chess because that is-- that was just-- there was about 20 of us, two dozen maybe, who were just assigned for this job.
And we were just more or less doing nothing.
I didn't realize at the time that people were-- people were starving of hunger.
People were being shot and killed.
I don't know what I would have done if I'd have known.
And they were working like Sunday.
Sunday was a day off.
Sunday afternoon.
They work six and a half days a week.
Sunday afternoon was off.
And we used to go like people go today to Hyde Park for a walk round up and down on the-- on the Appellplatz.
People used to go and nobody bothered you.
It was like a party more or less, Sunday afternoon.
It wasn't, of course, a party.
But they work six and a half days a week.
That's how it was in Buchenwald when I was there.
In fact, I was talking to a friend of mine, Morris Friedman.
And we worked it out the day they came and called my name how to take me off to another camp, he more or less took my bed.
And we sort of talked about it years later.
And we worked it out.
He says, I probably got your bed, the way I was describing it, next to the window, da da da, and so on and so on.
You know.
There were three bunks, and there was two people-- two people to a bed.
And there was-- there was not as like in-- in-- what can I say.
In Buchenwald, at the bottom part where there was these big blocks from 60 up.
There was bunk beds which we used to take four people at a time.
There was about 12 or 16, four-- four high.
But in-- once they sort of-- you went there for a sorting out, I would call it.
Because from there, they actually decided where to send you.
Because all the time, transports were arriving in Buchenwald.
From there, they sent different people to different-- other different works all over Germany.
And as I said, I was sent to-- to Block 8.
I didn't realize until years later that the block next to us was actually an experiment block.
They would experimenting with people, killing them, injecting them with all different poisons and all the rest.
This-- this is what I learned after reading books after.
But whilst I was there, I had no clue what was going on.
You see, they could-- they could kill people in one block, and then have a-- putting up a show for the Germans in another block.
They were crazy.
It was just craziness.
I remember one of my friends was telling me, there was a rumor that they were selecting people for the gas chambers.
And he was little.
And he didn't know what was happening.
People started running.
There was nowhere to run.
They spread.
And he stood his ground.
And a German saw him.
And he said to me, for the next six or eight weeks he was fed with white bread and salamis.
He come up and he said, Juden.
You was not afraid.
You stood your ground.
I watched you.
And that's how they were.
And-- and probably round the corner he was-- he was put-- putting cyanide in-- in the gas chambers, and-- and going home to his family like he was going home from any other job.
And yet he saw this friend of mine, free-- he froze.
He didn't move from his ground.
And he saw him, and he-- he said, he fed me and clothed me for four to six weeks.
White bread and salamis and all that, which you didn't see hardly ever.
That's how crazy they were.
Yeah.
That's how they were.
Yeah.
There's-- obviously age-- time makes you forget things.
Let's face it.
It's now 51 years since liberation, and this happened a year or two earlier.
So it's 52, 53 years.
And I remember things from then, and I don't remember what I did yesterday.
And then another day, I say, was that 50 years ago?
Seems like-- seems like a couple of years.
And-- and other times when something happened a couple years ago, you think it's happened 100 years ago.
Time plays games.
Time play-- yeah.
It does.
I was at Buchenwald nine months, till about Christmas time.
I did the same job all the time while I was there.
One night, I-- they called out about 17 numbers.
I was one of them.
They took us off to a clothing place.
We call it [GERMAN], used to be called.
And they told us to dress ourselves from top to bottom, whatever we fancy.
Whatever we want to fit ourselves out, complete new civilian clothes.
We did that.
We didn't know the reason why.
We didn't know how.
We didn't know nothing.
We didn't ask.
Nobody was there to be asked.
And I looked, you know, like a cheder boy.
Lovely and dressed and like going to-- ready for going to shul.
And from then on, we were actually taken at-- the following morning to the trains.
And the 17 of us got into a private train.
And what I mean by private train, it was an ordinary civilian train.
And I remember some people tried to get in.
And the-- one of the soldiers was saying, this is taken.
This [GERMAN].
And we were taken on this train.
We traveled about 14 hours.
Because they were-- had to stop a lot of times because of the bombings from the British and Americans on.
In fact, once when we arrived in Hanover, everybody out.
And we went in-- into bunkers.
And we held on to the greatcoats of the soldiers.
We didn't want to get lost.
We didn't know what was happening.
We didn't know what-- what's going on.
And we were holding on to them, you know.
You would think we would-- great.
We can escape.
There's bombing going on.
We were too young to know what was going on.
We was too young to know what's happening.
We going back to this.
Well we was in this train, we were fed food which I hadn't seen for donkey's years.
Like white bread and salamis and butter, if they-- or marg, or whatever it was.
And just had no idea what was happening, what was going on.
